topic.what: Narrative review examining how high dietary sugar intake shifts gut microbiota composition toward pro-inflammatory species, driving dysbiosis and gastrointestinal disease.
topic.how: Systematic review of mechanistic and epidemiological studies linking refined sugar and high-fructose corn syrup consumption to specific microbiome changes, increased intestinal permeability, endotoxemia, and downstream GI inflammation.
topic.why_matters: Identifies high sugar intake as a modifiable dietary driver of gut dysbiosis and inflammation, relevant to gastritis pathogenesis and the epidemiology of Western diet-associated GI disorders.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Strength of mechanistic evidence linking sugar consumption to pro-inflammatory microbiome shifts
characteristics.value: Multiple studies demonstrate high sugar intake selectively promotes Proteobacteria and reduces Bacteroidetes/Firmicutes beneficial species
characteristics.vs_baseline: Low-sugar or fiber-rich diets maintain higher microbiome diversity and anti-inflammatory species
characteristics.evidence: Review of animal and human observational studies; mechanistic pathway includes reduced mucosal IgA and increased LPS translocation
characteristics.context: Most mechanistic evidence from animal models; human studies are largely observational

characteristics.what_it_measures: Completeness of the sugar → dysbiosis → GI inflammation causal chain
characteristics.value: Pathway traced from sugar substrate selection → microbial community shift → increased gut permeability → LPS-mediated mucosal inflammation
characteristics.evidence: Synthesis of microbiome sequencing studies and mucosal biopsy data
characteristics.context: Causal inference limited by observational design of human studies; animal models support mechanistic links

good_for: Justifying dietary sugar reduction in gastritis management protocols
Understanding microbiome-mediated inflammation in Western diet-associated gastritis
Designing dietary intervention studies targeting sugar-induced dysbiosis
not_for: Establishing definitive causal thresholds for sugar intake and gastritis risk
Clinical treatment guidelines without additional RCT evidence
requires: Complementary RCT data to confirm causal relationships in human gastritis populations

novelty: Systematically links sugar-specific dietary patterns (rather than general Western diet) to pro-inflammatory microbiome shifts
Articulates a mechanistic chain from sugar intake through dysbiosis to mucosal inflammation relevant to GI disease
open_problems: Dose-response relationship between sugar intake and clinically significant dysbiosis remains unquantified
Whether sugar reduction alone (without fiber increase) suffices to reverse dysbiosis-associated gastric inflammation
Interaction effects between sugar type (sucrose, fructose, glucose) and microbiome composition need further study
